title: Hello, Fragment Shader
description: Every pixel on your screen can be any color — you decide. In a fragment shader, your job is to output exactly one `vec4` color (R, G, B, Alpha) for each fragment. Let's start by painting the entire canvas a solid color.

theory.key_concept: Fragment shaders run once per pixel. Your only required output is a color — a `vec4` with components in the range [0.0, 1.0]. The renderer prepends `#version 300 es` and `precision highp float;` automatically.
theory.glsl_notes: In GLSL ES 3.0, you declare a named output variable instead of writing to `gl_FragColor`. Use `out vec4 fragColor;` at the top of your shader, then assign to it inside `main()`.
theory.color_model: Colors are (Red, Green, Blue, Alpha). Each component is a float from 0.0 (none) to 1.0 (full). Alpha controls transparency but is typically 1.0 for opaque output.

challenges.title: Paint it red
challenges.description: Output a solid red color for every pixel. Red is full red channel (1.0), no green, no blue, fully opaque.
hints: A `vec4` takes four float arguments: (R, G, B, A). For pure red with full opacity, only the first component should be 1.0.
Try `vec4(1.0, 0.0, 0.0, 1.0)` — that is full red, no green, no blue, fully opaque.
challenges.unlocks_theory: Colors as vectors: each channel is an independent scalar in [0,1]. Mixing colors is just adding scaled vectors.

hlsl.note: In HLSL the entry point returns a value instead of writing to a declared output variable.

slang.note: Slang uses HLSL-style syntax. The `[shader("fragment")]` attribute marks the entry point.

challenges.title: Mix your own color
challenges.description: Paint the canvas a warm orange. Orange lives between red (1, 0, 0) and yellow (1, 1, 0) — a high red, medium green, and very low blue.
hints: Think of orange as red with a bit of green mixed in. Keep red at 1.0, set green somewhere around 0.4–0.6, and keep blue very low (0.0–0.15).
Try `vec4(1.0, 0.5, 0.1, 1.0)`. Experiment — there is no single correct orange. The validator accepts any warm orange in that neighbourhood.

sample_points.note: Generous tolerance — any warm orange accepted

challenges.unlocks_theory: The RGB colour cube: moving along the R axis gives reds, adding G creates yellows and oranges, adding B pushes towards white.

challenges.title: Use a uniform
challenges.description: The renderer supplies `u_resolution` — a `vec2` containing the canvas width and height in pixels. Use it to derive the red channel so the color reacts to the canvas size.
hints: Uniforms are read-only values passed from the CPU. `u_resolution.x` is the canvas width in pixels — divide it by 1000.0 to bring it into the [0, 1] range for a typical canvas.
Assign `u_resolution.x / 1000.0` to `r`, then pass `r` as the first component of `fragColor`.
challenges.unlocks_theory: Uniforms are the CPU-to-GPU data channel. Common uniforms: `u_time` (seconds since start), `u_resolution` (canvas size), `u_mouse` (cursor position). You declare them in the shader and the engine fills them in each frame.

hlsl.note: In HLSL, uniforms live in a constant buffer. The `cbuffer` block is bound by the engine.

slang.note: Slang supports both HLSL constant buffers and a cleaner `ParameterBlock` pattern.
